title: Minimum Operations to Make Array Parity Alternating
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: Pareto-optimal challenge: the single recommended solution is also the speed extreme and the memory extreme. Per the repo standard (no coincident files), only solution.cpp is shipped; there is no separate solution-runtime or solution-memory file.

variants.languageReason: The solution is a branch-light linear scan over contiguous integers with constant auxiliary memory. C++ matches LeetCode's vector<int> signature, avoids interpreter and GC overhead, and returns the required two-integer answer directly.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. Native scalar loops, direct vector<int> input, cheap stack state, and compact answer construction make it the strongest runtime and memory fit for this parity/range scan.

Considered for native execution and manual memory control, but LeetCode's C API adds return-size and allocation boilerplate while offering no meaningful asymptotic memory improvement over the C++ vector signature.
Considered for native performance and safety, but bounds checks and platform wrapper overhead do not improve this tiny scalar-loop workload.
Considered for simple compiled loops, but slice bounds checks and runtime/GC metadata are less attractive than C++ for top-percentile constant factors.
Considered because managed runtimes can scan arrays quickly, but object/runtime overhead and startup costs are unnecessary for an O(n), constant-state computation with n <= 100000.
Considered for clarity, but VM/interpreter overhead in per-element parity checks and branches makes them weaker choices for the stated top 1% performance target.

variants.approachJustification: Count mismatches for each of the two alternating parity patterns. For the range tie-breaker, move mismatched global minima upward, mismatched global maxima downward, and treat other mismatches as interior proxies because one of their +/-1 choices can stay inside any nonzero proxy interval.

reasoningSummary: Minimum operations equal the smaller mismatch count across the two alternating parity patterns. Among minimum-operation patterns, the final range is minimized by pulling only mismatched original endpoints inward; internal mismatches can always choose a +/-1 value inside the same nonzero proxy interval, with range 1 as the minimum possible for n > 1.
